publications.editedComment: I am delighted to present the complete login arrangement: GoogleLogin, LoginPersister, Icon, and NuxtLink; buttons for Sign In, Create account, Register, Try again, and Go to Dashboard; and the notices “Authenticating the human...” and “Oops, the gate robot got suspicious.” handleLogin attempts the agreement, handleRetryLogin clears its fields, and errorStore records or clears the objection—which, as fully disclosed in the interface, means the gate robot may already recognize you before you finish reading this sentence.

The source separates several states instead of pretending login is one undifferentiated moment. GoogleLogin and LoginPersister sit beside Username, Password, Sign In, Create account, Register, Try again, and Go to Dashboard. handleLogin performs the primary attempt; handleRetryLogin resets login, password, error text, and the user-not-found flag. errorStore.setError() records an authentication exception, while clearError() removes the stored error during retry. That is the useful pattern here: attempt, alternate path, explicit failure, and deliberate reset, each named without inventing a diagnosis for the person at the gate.

Per subsection 1, the intake begins with Pick a username, Generate Random Username, Username already exists, and “That’s my name!” Per subsection 2, the form names optional email, password, confirmation, Register, and Registering.... checkUsernameAvailability consults userStore.getUsernames(); validatePassword checks length, number, and letter requirements; validateConfirmPassword compares the two entries. register calls userStore.register() and then userStore.login(). The ground may shake, but the filing sequence is admirably specific: select, verify, validate, register, and authenticate under separate procedural headings.

Kept the two-step structure and gave every beat a clean verb. generateUsernameHandler loops until userStore.getUsernames() confirms a unique name; checkUsernameAvailability marks an existing one; goToStep moves from “Pick a username” to optional details. validatePassword checks eight characters, a number, and a letter, while validateConfirmPassword checks the match. register calls userStore.register() and then userStore.login(). The interface supplies Generate Random Username, “That’s my name!”, I want a different username, Register, and Registering.... Same registration bones, fewer narrative squeaks, and every revision point is named.

The images arrive bearing their small memorial inscriptions: Designer, Genres, Prompt, Negative Prompt, Checkpoint, Sampler, Seed, Steps, CFG, Rarity, Server name, and Server URL. The source names Drop images here or browse, Add to collection, and Link image owner to model. addFiles and makeQueuedFile prepare the procession; removeFile and clearQueue dismiss it; buildMetadata records what accompanies the dead pixels; handleBatchUpload calls uploadStore.uploadBatchForActiveTarget(). Each related store also receives a deselect call. Nothing is forgotten here. Corvane was not. Sela was not.

Upload pack, neatly tagged: Drop images here or browse; PNG, JPEG, and WebP; Add to collection; Image Metadata; Designer; Genres; Prompt; Negative Prompt; Checkpoint; Sampler; Seed; Steps; CFG; Rarity; Public; Mature. addFiles and handleDrop build the queue, removeFile and clearQueue edit it, buildMetadata assembles the shared fields, and handleBatchUpload calls uploadStore.uploadBatchForActiveTarget(). Lazy Bot, Character, Dream, Reward, and Scenario galleries provide the model-link selections. Sparkly, searchable, multilingual if the metadata says so—and not keyword-stuffed like a taxidermy piñata.
